fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Experience with formalising classical mathematics in both Isabelle/HOL and Lean suggests that indefinite description is a very convenient feature to have in a language, not least because it greatly simplifies the treatment of partial functions as Larry Paulson has pointed out. For example, you can define division in Isabelle as a total function by sending n/0 to \mathsf { some } _{ \mathsf { nat } }. This function is perfectly well-defined, and avoids the frustration of needing to check that arguments are non-zero when you apply it — or the (dual) frustration of needing to check that the output is defined before you use it, as in accounts of partiality based on partial map classifier monads.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Indefinite description in homotopy type theory

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Certain foundational systems for mathematics employ global choice or indefinite description operators; operators of this kind are assumed by Bourbaki, Isabelle/HOL, and Lean. Indefinite description is similar to the axiom of choice, but quite a bit stronger:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \frac { \Gamma , x:A \mid \phi { \mathopen {} \left ( x \right ) \mathclose {}} : \mathsf { prop } \qquad \Gamma \vdash \exists x : A. \top }{ \Gamma \mid { \mathopen {} \left ( \epsilon x:A. \phi { \mathopen {} \left ( x \right ) \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} : A \qquad \Gamma ,z:A \mid \phi { \mathopen {} \left ( z \right ) \mathclose {}} \vdash \phi { \mathopen {} \left ( \epsilon x:A. \phi { \mathopen {} \left ( x \right ) \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} }
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: In the presence of subset types, indefinite description in the sense above is equivalent to the presence of a single global choice operator:

Experience with formalising classical mathematics in both Isabelle/HOL and Lean suggests that indefinite description is a very convenient feature to have in a language, not least because it greatly simplifies the treatment of partial functions as Larry Paulson has pointed out. For example, you can define division in Isabelle as a total function by sending n/0 to \mathsf { some } _{ \mathsf { nat } }. This function is perfectly well-defined, and avoids the frustration of needing to check that arguments are non-zero when you apply it — or the (dual) frustration of needing to check that the output is defined before you use it, as in accounts of partiality based on partial map classifier monads.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Indefinite description is inconsistent with HoTT/UF

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Unfortunately, global choice and indefinite description are inconsistent with homotopy type theory / univalent foundations, as they imply that every type is a set. In fact, as can be seen from the proof of Theorem 3.2.2 of the HoTT Book, it is even inconsistent with univalent foundations to assume global choice only for sets!

To express classical mathematics in univalent foundations, it is therefore necessary to use the more cumbersome axiom of choice — which is strictly weaker than global choice. When { \mathopen {} \left \lVert - \right \rVert \mathclose {}} is the propositional truncation operator, global choice would be witnessed by a family of functions \mathsf { some } _A : { \mathopen {} \left \lVert A \right \rVert \mathclose {}} \to A whereas the axiom of choice is witnessed by a family of functions \mathsf { AC } _{ A,B } : { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } } { \mathopen {} \left \lVert B { \mathopen {} \left ( x \right ) \mathclose {}} \right \rVert \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} \to { \mathopen {} \left \lVert \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } } B { \mathopen {} \left ( x \right ) \mathclose {}} \right \rVert \mathclose {}}. The crucial difference is the presence of propositional truncation in the output.

I believe that it is nonetheless possible to have the “best of both worlds” and elaborate a high-level language with indefinite descriptions to homotopy type theory with the axiom of choice. The idea has two main parts:

fr:ol.fr:li: First of all, we must interpret the “signatures” of top-level declarations as being fully abstract, taking place in the propositional truncation monad. For instance, a definitional extension \mathsf { def } \, t : A : \equiv u should be interpreted as producing an element of the truncation { \mathopen {} \left \lVert \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {}} } } x = u \right \rVert \mathclose {}}; subsequent declarations proceed by unpacking this truncation using the contractibility of singletons.

Secondly, we shall elaborate an occurrence of \Gamma \vdash \mathsf { some } _A { \mathopen {} \left ( u \right ) \mathclose {}} :A given \Gamma \vdash u : { \mathopen {} \left \lVert A \right \rVert \mathclose {}} by unleashing a new top-level declaration \vartheta : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( \cdots : \Gamma \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( \cdots : \Gamma \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( \cdots : \Gamma \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( \cdots : \Gamma \right ) \mathclose {}} } } A, which (under the interpretation of top-level declarations via truncation) can be defined from \lambda \cdots .u using the ordinary axiom of choice. Then \mathsf { some } (u) is elaborated to \vartheta { \mathopen {} \left ( \cdots \right ) \mathclose {}}.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Thus we have a language for univalent foundations in which indefinite descriptions can be used. There is a minor trade-off in the proposed treatment, however: two occurrences of \mathsf { some } _A { \mathopen {} \left ( u \right ) \mathclose {}} will elaborate to different core language terms. It may be possible to get around this by means of some kind of caching, but I think that may be ultimately unnecessary in practice — as the entire point of an indefinite description is that it shall satisfy no properties except the specified one.
